title: Eigenmodes of synthetic antiferromagnetic skyrmions
authors: Kauser Zulfiqar, Martin Lang, Samuel J. R. Holt, Swapneel Amit Pathak, Florian Bruckner, Hans Fangohr
abstract: We investigate the excitation modes of confined synthetic-antiferromagnetic (SAF) skyrmions using micromagnetic eigenvalue and ringdown simulations. Starting from a single skyrmion in a ferromagnetic layer, where the lowest-frequency modes are a gyrotropic and a breathing mode, we study how antiferromagnetic interlayer coupling modifies the dynamics in SAF bilayers. We consider several geometries: single SAF skyrmions in square and rectangular confinement, unequal layer thicknesses, and strips containing multiple skyrmions.   The antiferromagnetic coupling strongly modifies the low-frequency dynamics. The square geometry exhibits two nearly degenerate gyrotropic modes, where in each both layers have the same rotation sense. In rectangular geometries, we instead find nearly linear SAF skyrmion translation emerging from opposite gyration sense in the two layers. These translational modes become the characteristic low-frequency excitations of SAF skyrmion chains.   For skyrmion chains, we identify collective translational and breathing modes with standing-wave-like spatial profiles. Beyond ferromagnetic-like breathing modes, the SAF geometry supports breathing oscillations in which the two layers oscillate out of phase. We further demonstrate signal propagation along extended SAF skyrmion chains with propagation velocities comparable to ferromagnetic skyrmion chains.   These results provide a systematic description of the collective dynamics of SAF skyrmions arising from the interplay of geometric confinement, intralayer, and interlayer coupling.
summary: This work characterizes the magnetic excitation modes of skyrmions confined in synthetic antiferromagnetic bilayers. By using advanced micromagnetic simulations, the authors show that interlayer coupling fundamentally alters low-frequency dynamics, revealing unique translational and out-of-phase breathing modes. The research provides a systematic understanding of collective excitations and signal propagation in these coupled magnetic systems.
main_problem: The study investigates the excitation modes and collective dynamics of skyrmions confined within synthetic antiferromagnetic (SAF) bilayers.
main_result: The SAF interlayer coupling strongly modifies low-frequency excitations, leading to distinct translational and out-of-phase breathing modes compared to ferromagnetic systems. Signal propagation along extended chains is also demonstrated.
method: Micromagnetic simulations are employed using both the eigenvalue method (for normal modes) and ringdown methods (for full dynamics). The analysis involves solving linearized LLG equations for small fluctuations.
model_or_system: The system models SAF bilayers, consisting of two coupled ferromagnetic layers hosting single or multiple skyrmions in various geometries (square, rectangular, chains). Dynamics are governed by the interplay between geometric confinement and interlayer coupling.
key_observables: Normal mode frequencies ($\omega_k$), Power Spectral Density (PSD), gyrotropic/translational/breathing modes, and signal propagation velocities.
important_parameters: Interlayer coupling strength ($\sigma$), sample geometry aspect ratio ($L_x/L_y$), and the number of skyrmions in a chain.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on linear response theory for small oscillations; specific approximations are made regarding power spectrum calculation (e.g., neglecting off-diagonal terms).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate PSDs, normal mode phase/amplitude maps for single and multiple skyrmions in SAF geometries, and time evolution showing signal propagation along chains.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the dynamics by first establishing the theoretical framework (eigenmode solvers), then applying it to single skyrmions in various confined geometries, followed by analyzing collective modes in extended skyrmion chains, and finally demonstrating signal propagation.
why_interesting: While focused on magnetism, the rigorous application of linear response theory, generalized eigenvalue problems, and time-domain simulation techniques for characterizing collective excitations is highly relevant to open quantum systems dynamics and wave phenomena in structured media.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of geometries and skyrmion distribution studied in this work. Colour indicates the mz component of the magnetisation (red mz = +1, blue mz = −1, grey mz = 0). The skyrmions are schematically sketched on the top surface, they extend through the whole thickness. The skyrmion core polarisation is always opposite to the background magnetisation direction, i.e. flips at the layer interface for those systems where the background magnetisation is flipped.

Figure 2a shows the power spectral density (PSD) for a Néel skyrmion in a (chiral) ferromagnetic material (geometry Fig. 1a, equilibrium configuration shown in Fig. 2b). The dashed lines show the PSD obtained from the ringdown simulations. Solid lines show spectra computed from the eigenvalues obtained with the eigenvalue method (see Methods for details how the PSD is calculated).

Figure 2. (a) Power spectral density (PSD) of Néel skyrmion in xy plane for two different excitations. An in-plane excitation field acting in the x direction excites modes A and C, an out-of-plane excitation field acting in the z direction excites mode B. The solid lines show the spectra computed from the eigenvalue method, the dashed lines the spectra obtained from the ringdown method. (b) Equilibrium configuration and first three eigenmodes A, B and C of a Néel skyrmion with core pointing in the −z direction (Fig. 1a). (c) Equilibrium configuration and first three eigenmodes A, B, and C of a Néel skyrmion with core pointing in the +z direction (Fig. 1b).

Figure 3. (a) Power spectral density and (b) normal modes for a SAF skyrmion (geometry as in Fig. 1c). The Néel skyrmion in the top layer has the vortex core pointing in −z direction, and the skyrmion in the bottom layer has the vortex core pointing in the +z direction. The layers are antiferromagnetically coupled. (a) The blue line shows the power spectral density for out- of-plane excitation acting in the z-direction. The orange line shows the power spectral density for an excitation acting in the in-plane x-direction. (b) The observed normal modes are gyrotropic (A and B) and breathing modes (C and D). (c) Gyrotropic normal modes and frequencies for a SAF skyrmion in square geometry with...

Figure 4. (a) PSD and (b) lowest frequency normal modes of a skyrmion in a SAF with sample dimensions Lx = 32 nm and Ly = 40 nm. We classify modes A and B as translational modes, and modes C and D as breathing modes. For more details refer to the main text.
